analogy: Think of "Structural Organisation in Animals" through the chapter's own opening images: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 In the preceding chapters you came across a large variety of organisms, both unicellular and multicellular, of the animal kingdom. In unicellular organisms, all functions like digestion, respiration and reproduction are performed by a single cell. In the complex body... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79, 7.1 ORGAN AND ORGAN SYSTEM, STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN, ANIMALS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Structural Organisation in Animals is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that sTRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 In the preceding chapters you came across a large variety of organisms, both unicellular and multicellular, of the animal kingdom.

The text explains that in unicellular organisms, all functions like digestion, respiration and reproduction are performed by a single cell.

The text explains that in the complex body of multicellular animals the same basic functions are carried out by different groups of cells in a well organised manner.

The text explains that the body of a simple organism like Hydra is made of different types of cells and the number of cells in each type can be in thousands.

The text explains that the human body is composed of billions of cells to perform various functions.

The text explains that how do these cells in the body work together?

The text explains that as you have already learnt in your earlier classes, in multicellular animals, a group of similar cells alongwith intercellular substances perform a specific function.

The text explains that you may be surprised to know that all complex animals consist of only four basic types of tissues.

The text explains that these tissues are organised in specific proportion and pattern to form an organ like stomach, lung, heart and kidney.

The text explains that when two or more organs perform a common function by their physical and/or chemical interaction, they together form organ system, e.g., digestive system, respiratory system, etc.

The text explains that cells, tissues, organs and organ systems split up the work in a way that exhibits division of labour and contribute to the survival of the body as a whole.

The text explains that 7.1 ORGAN AND ORGAN SYSTEM The basic tissues as you have learnt in earlier classes, organise to form organs which in turn associate to form organ systems in the multicellular organisms.

The text explains that such an organisation is essential for more efficient and better coordinated activities of millions of cells constituting an organism.

The text explains that each STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS CHAPTER 7 7.1 Organ and Organ System 7.2 Frogs

The text explains that 80 BIOLOGY organ in our body is made of one or more type of tissues.

The text explains that for example, our heart consists of all the four types of tissues, i.e., epithelial, connective, muscular and neural.

The text explains that we also notice, after some careful study that the complexity in organ and organ systems displays certain discernable trend.

The text explains that in this chapter, you are being introduced to morphology and anatomy of frog.

The text explains that morphology refers to study of form or externally visible features.

The text explains that they do not have constant body temperature i.e., their body temperature varies with the temperature of the environment.

The text explains that the forelimbs and hind limbs help in Figure 7.1 External features of frog Eye Fore limb Hind limb Head Trunk

The text explains that 7.2.2 Anatomy The body cavity of frogs accommodate different organ systems such as digestive, circulatory, respiratory, nervous, excretory and reproductive systems with well developed structures and functions (Figure 7.2).

The text explains that the digestive system consists of alimentary canal and digestive glands.

The text explains that pancreas, a digestive gland produces pancreatic juice Figure 7.2 Diagrammatic representation of internal organs of frog showing complete digestive system Intestine Ureter Rectum Cloaca Urinary bladder Oesophagus Liver Stomach Kidney Gall bladder Fat bodies Lung Heart Cloacal Aperture

The text explains that the lungs are a pair of elongated, pink coloured sac-like structures present in the upper part of the trunk region (thorax).

The text explains that the vascular system of frog is well-developed closed type.

The text explains that the blood vascular system involves heart, blood vessels and blood.

The text explains that the lymphatic system consists of lymph, lymph channels and lymph nodes.

The text explains that heart is a muscular structure situated in the upper part of the body cavity.

The text explains that a triangular structure called sinus venosus joins the right atrium.

The text explains that the blood from the heart is carried to all parts of the body by the arteries (arterial system).

The text explains that the veins collect blood from different parts of body to the heart and form the venous system.

The text explains that the former is called hepatic portal system and the latter is called renal portal system.

The text explains that the elimination of nitrogenous wastes is carried out by a well developed excretory system.

The text explains that the excretory system consists of a pair of kidneys, ureters, cloaca and urinary bladder.

The text explains that these are compact, dark red and bean like structures situated a little posteriorly in the body cavity on both sides of vertebral column.

The text explains that the system for control and coordination is highly evolved in the frog.

The text explains that it includes both neural system and endocrine glands.

The text explains that the nervous system is organised into a central nervous system (brain and spinal cord), a peripheral nervous system (cranial and spinal nerves) and an autonomic nervous system (sympathetic and parasympathetic).

The text explains that brain is enclosed in a bony structure called brain box (cranium).

The text explains that out of these, eyes and internal ears are well-organised structures and the rest are cellular aggregations around nerve endings.

The text explains that eyes in a frog are a pair of spherical structures situated in the orbit in skull.

The text explains that frogs have well organised male and female reproductive systems.

The text explains that they enter the kidneys on their side and open into Bidder’s Figure 7.3 Male reproductive system Fat bodies Kidney Urino genital duct Cloaca Cloacal aperture Testis Adrenal gland Urinary bladder Rectum Vasa efferentia Figure 7.4 Female reproductive system Oviduct Ovary Ova Ureter Cloaca Cloacal aperture Urinary bladder

The text explains that frogs maintain ecological balance because these serve as an important link of food chain and food web in the ecosystem.

The text explains that sUMMARY Cells, tissues, organs and organ systems split up the work in a way that ensures the survival of the body as a whole and exhibit division of labour.

The text explains that circulatory system is closed with single circulation.

The text explains that the main digestive glands are liver and pancreas.

The text explains that it can respire in water through skin and through lungs on land.

The text explains that nervous system is organised into central, peripheral and autonomic.

The text explains that the organs of urinogenital system are kidneys and urinogenital ducts, which open into the cloaca.

The text explains that the male reproductive organ is a pair of testes.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7: Structural Organisation in Animals
